SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes PTK and cPTK and presents empirical results on Twitter15/16 showing improved accuracy, so the main empirical claim is partially supported. However, from the summary there are no formal properties, consistency results, or complexity bounds for the proposed kernels, no hyperparameter reporting or code, and no mention of statistical significance or robust ablations that isolate the propagation-structure contribution. These missing theoretical guarantees, runtime/scalability analysis, and stronger controlled experiments constitute notable gaps that prevent a higher technical score. | B(3): The paper’s main claims are plausible and supported by empirical comparisons on two real-world datasets (Twitter15/16) showing PTK/cPTK + SVM outperform several baselines including a GRU, which indicates the proposed kernels capture useful propagation structure. However, important methodological details are missing: hyperparameters are not reported, code is unavailable, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, which undermines reproducibility and confidence in the reported improvements. The authors also exclude unpopular source tweets (potential sampling bias) and construct their own propagation-tree datasets, introducing an unsupported assumption about generality. Overall, the technical approach appears sound but the experimental validation and reproducibility support are insufficient to fully trust all claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes PTK and cPTK and presents empirical results on Twitter15/16 showing improved accuracy, so the main empirical claim is partially supported. However, from the summary there are no formal properties, consistency results, or complexity bounds for the proposed kernels, no hyperparameter reporting or code, and no mention of statistical significance or robust ablations that isolate the propagation-structure contribution. These missing theoretical guarantees, runtime/scalability analysis, and stronger controlled experiments constitute notable gaps that prevent a higher technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are plausible and supported by empirical comparisons on two real-world datasets (Twitter15/16) showing PTK/cPTK + SVM outperform several baselines including a GRU, which indicates the proposed kernels capture useful propagation structure. However, important methodological details are missing: hyperparameters are not reported, code is unavailable, and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, which undermines reproducibility and confidence in the reported improvements. The authors also exclude unpopular source tweets (potential sampling bias) and construct their own propagation-tree datasets, introducing an unsupported assumption about generality. Overall, the technical approach appears sound but the experimental validation and reproducibility support are insufficient to fully trust all claims.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem (rumor detection) and proposes a clear modeling contribution (Propagation Tree Kernel and context-sensitive cPTK) that empirically outperforms several baselines on two real-world Twitter datasets. However, from my theory-first perspective the impact is limited: there is no reported theoretical analysis (e.g., kernel properties, complexity or scalability guarantees), no code/hyperparameter transparency, and the evaluation is confined to two datasets with constructed propagation trees and exclusion of unpopular tweets—limitations that curb broad adoption. Thus the work is useful and likely to spur follow-up work on propagation-aware methods, but it is not yet positioned to strongly shift research or practice without further theoretical and empirical validation. | B(3): This paper tackles an important applied problem—rumor detection on social media—by explicitly modeling propagation trees via a novel propagation tree kernel (PTK) and a context-sensitive variant (cPTK), and demonstrates improved early-detection accuracy on real-world Twitter15/16 datasets. From an applied-NLP/deployment perspective the idea of leveraging diffusion structure is practically valuable and could influence practitioners who need early signals, but the impact is moderated by limited baseline breadth (no deep structural SOTA beyond a single GRU), lack of reported hyperparameters/code, and the focus on highly retweeted posts which reduces generality. Overall the work is a useful, empirically-driven contribution that should stimulate follow-up research on structured propagation models, but it is unlikely to be transformative without stronger reproducibility and broader evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem (rumor detection) and proposes a clear modeling contribution (Propagation Tree Kernel and context-sensitive cPTK) that empirically outperforms several baselines on two real-world Twitter datasets. However, from my theory-first perspective the impact is limited: there is no reported theoretical analysis (e.g., kernel properties, complexity or scalability guarantees), no code/hyperparameter transparency, and the evaluation is confined to two datasets with constructed propagation trees and exclusion of unpopular tweets—limitations that curb broad adoption. Thus the work is useful and likely to spur follow-up work on propagation-aware methods, but it is not yet positioned to strongly shift research or practice without further theoretical and empirical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important applied problem—rumor detection on social media—by explicitly modeling propagation trees via a novel propagation tree kernel (PTK) and a context-sensitive variant (cPTK), and demonstrates improved early-detection accuracy on real-world Twitter15/16 datasets. From an applied-NLP/deployment perspective the idea of leveraging diffusion structure is practically valuable and could influence practitioners who need early signals, but the impact is moderated by limited baseline breadth (no deep structural SOTA beyond a single GRU), lack of reported hyperparameters/code, and the focus on highly retweeted posts which reduces generality. Overall the work is a useful, empirically-driven contribution that should stimulate follow-up research on structured propagation models, but it is unlikely to be transformative without stronger reproducibility and broader evaluation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a clearly relevant ML/NLP topic (rumor detection on Twitter) and proposes a kernel-based method (PTK/cPTK) integrated with SVMs, which fits the audience of top venues that accept algorithmic contributions and applied social-media modeling. However, from my theory-first perspective there are scope concerns: the summary reports no formal properties or theoretical guarantees for the proposed kernel, no hyperparameter reporting, no code, and no runtime/scalability analysis, and evaluation is limited to two datasets (Twitter15/16) without robustness tests. These omissions reduce its suitability for a top-tier ML conference unless augmented with at least minimal theoretical characterization, controlled ablations, and empirical scalability/robustness analysis. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (rumor detection) and proposes a structured propagation-tree kernel (PTK/cPTK) with evaluation on standard Twitter15/16 datasets, so the topic and audience fit top-tier venues. The empirical setup compares against multiple baselines (including GRU and several classical classifiers) and claims improved early detection, which aligns with applied community expectations. I lower one point because reproducibility details are missing (no code or hyperparameter reporting) and the authors limit scope to highly-retweeted posts, which raises questions about generalizability and practical deployment.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a clearly relevant ML/NLP topic (rumor detection on Twitter) and proposes a kernel-based method (PTK/cPTK) integrated with SVMs, which fits the audience of top venues that accept algorithmic contributions and applied social-media modeling. However, from my theory-first perspective there are scope concerns: the summary reports no formal properties or theoretical guarantees for the proposed kernel, no hyperparameter reporting, no code, and no runtime/scalability analysis, and evaluation is limited to two datasets (Twitter15/16) without robustness tests. These omissions reduce its suitability for a top-tier ML conference unless augmented with at least minimal theoretical characterization, controlled ablations, and empirical scalability/robustness analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (rumor detection) and proposes a structured propagation-tree kernel (PTK/cPTK) with evaluation on standard Twitter15/16 datasets, so the topic and audience fit top-tier venues. The empirical setup compares against multiple baselines (including GRU and several classical classifiers) and claims improved early detection, which aligns with applied community expectations. I lower one point because reproducibility details are missing (no code or hyperparameter reporting) and the authors limit scope to highly-retweeted posts, which raises questions about generalizability and practical deployment.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (PTK and cPTK integrated with SVM) are stated early and communicated clearly; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and method sections appear consistent enough to follow the propagation-tree formulation, and experiments follow a logical flow from methodology to empirical comparison on Twitter15/Twitter16. Presentation has minor omissions relevant to reproducibility and clarity (no code mentioned, hyperparameters not reported, and the constructed datasets are noted but not accompanied by public release), which prevent a top score. Figures/tables effectiveness is not clearly reported in the summary, so I withhold judgment there. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (PTK and cPTK for propagation-tree-based rumor detection) are stated early and the writing quality is described as good. Related work coverage is thorough and the experimental setup and datasets are clearly identified, supporting a logical flow from method to experiments to conclusion. Presentation has minor omissions: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which hurts reproducibility and reduces clarity for readers attempting to replicate results. Also the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables and notation are fully detailed, so while the paper reads well, these presentation gaps prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (PTK and cPTK integrated with SVM) are stated early and communicated clearly; writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and method sections appear consistent enough to follow the propagation-tree formulation, and experiments follow a logical flow from methodology to empirical comparison on Twitter15/Twitter16. Presentation has minor omissions relevant to reproducibility and clarity (no code mentioned, hyperparameters not reported, and the constructed datasets are noted but not accompanied by public release), which prevent a top score. Figures/tables effectiveness is not clearly reported in the summary, so I withhold judgment there.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (PTK and cPTK for propagation-tree-based rumor detection) are stated early and the writing quality is described as good. Related work coverage is thorough and the experimental setup and datasets are clearly identified, supporting a logical flow from method to experiments to conclusion. Presentation has minor omissions: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which hurts reproducibility and reduces clarity for readers attempting to replicate results. Also the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables and notation are fully detailed, so while the paper reads well, these presentation gaps prevent a top score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experimental evaluation uses two real-world datasets (Twitter15/16) and compares against several baselines, but it lacks important robustness checks (e.g., statistical significance, runtime scaling) and theoretical analysis that I expect. Ablation or sensitivity analyses are not reported (no hyperparameters disclosed and no controlled study isolating cPTK vs PTK contributions), so claimed novelty isn't isolated. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the authors do acknowledge some dataset/selection limitations, but reproducibility is weak (no code or hyperparameter details, bespoke dataset construction). Overall the paper provides adequate empirical evidence but misses several key analyses required for strong methodological validation. | B(3): The paper evaluates on two real-world benchmarks (Twitter15/16) and compares against multiple baselines (SVM-TS, DTR, DTC, SVM-RBF, RFC, GRU), which supports the core accuracy claims. However, the summary lists only accuracy (no early-detection/runtime numbers despite claims of quicker detection), and there is no mention of ablation/sensitivity studies or reported hyperparameters/code for reproducibility. The authors do acknowledge dataset construction and selection limitations, but missing training details and deeper analyses (e.g., ablations of PTK vs cPTK, robustness to unpopular tweets) keep this from being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation uses two real-world datasets (Twitter15/16) and compares against several baselines, but it lacks important robustness checks (e.g., statistical significance, runtime scaling) and theoretical analysis that I expect. Ablation or sensitivity analyses are not reported (no hyperparameters disclosed and no controlled study isolating cPTK vs PTK contributions), so claimed novelty isn't isolated. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the authors do acknowledge some dataset/selection limitations, but reproducibility is weak (no code or hyperparameter details, bespoke dataset construction). Overall the paper provides adequate empirical evidence but misses several key analyses required for strong methodological validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on two real-world benchmarks (Twitter15/16) and compares against multiple baselines (SVM-TS, DTR, DTC, SVM-RBF, RFC, GRU), which supports the core accuracy claims. However, the summary lists only accuracy (no early-detection/runtime numbers despite claims of quicker detection), and there is no mention of ablation/sensitivity studies or reported hyperparameters/code for reproducibility. The authors do acknowledge dataset construction and selection limitations, but missing training details and deeper analyses (e.g., ablations of PTK vs cPTK, robustness to unpopular tweets) keep this from being fully comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a concrete methodological contribution by introducing propagation tree kernels (PTK/cPTK) for rumor detection, and that this is a meaningful practical advance over propagation-agnostic baselines. However, they also agree the idea is an incremental extension of existing tree/kernel methods rather than a radical new paradigm, and the work lacks formal properties or theoretical analysis that would push it into a higher originality rating. Given the solid applied novelty but limited theoretical innovation, a moderate score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a specific new similarity measure (Propagation Tree Kernel and its context-sensitive variant) applied to rumor detection, which is a concrete methodological contribution beyond off-the-shelf classifiers. However, the idea of using tree kernels or propagation-structure similarity is a natural extension of existing tree/kernel methods rather than a radical new paradigm, and the summary lists no formal properties, complexity analysis, or theoretical guarantees that would elevate it to high originality. Given the application-focused evaluation on two datasets and use of an SVM wrapper, the contribution reads as a moderate, non-trivial extension rather than a highly novel idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a focused methodological contribution — a propagation tree kernel (PTK) and a context-sensitive variant (cPTK) for modeling diffusion trees in rumor detection — which is a clear, concrete new approach beyond standard feature/SVM or sequence/GRU baselines. Framed as a kernel-SVM pipeline and evaluated on Twitter15/16, the work differentiates itself from prior classifiers and propagation-agnostic models and introduces a non-trivial specialized similarity measure over tree structures. From my applied-NLP perspective this is a notable, practical novelty (not a wholly radical paradigm shift), and would likely inform experts working on structure-aware misinformation detection.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper uses standard benchmarks and a reasonable set of classical baselines, so comparisons are broadly useful, but they also agree important weaknesses remain: missing more recent propagation-aware competitors (e.g., GNNs), lack of hyperparameter details/code, and absence of statistical tests or ablations to isolate the claimed contribution. Given these omissions that weaken fairness and reproducibility, a moderate score of 3 reflects partly meaningful but incomplete comparisons.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates PTK/cPTK on two standard datasets (Twitter15/16) and against a set of classical baselines (SVM-TS, DTR, DTC, SVM-RBF, RFC) and a GRU, so it provides some reasonable comparisons. However, the summary indicates only accuracy is reported, hyperparameters are not disclosed, and there is no mention of statistical tests or ablations that would isolate the propagation-structure contribution; the authors also claim superiority rather than acknowledging when baselines perform comparably. From a theory-first perspective this yields some useful empirical evidence but falls short of comprehensive, fully transparent and controlled comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on standard benchmarks (Twitter15/16) and compares against a range of classical and neural baselines (SVM-TS, DTR, DTC, SVM-RBF, RFC, GRU) using the same metric (accuracy), so comparisons appear largely fair. However, it omits more recent and directly relevant propagation/graph-based neural approaches (e.g., GNNs or other propagation-aware deep models) that would be natural competitors to a propagation-structure kernel; the manuscript also does not report hyperparameters or release code which weakens reproducibility of the comparisons. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the empirical baseline set has minor omissions, so I rate it a 4.

main_claims: Proposes Propagation Tree Kernel (PTK) to model propagation structures of microblog posts for rumor detection.
Introduces a context-sensitive variant cPTK that captures propagation paths for improved discrimination.
Shows that the kernel-based PTK/cPTK integrated with an SVM can detect rumors more quickly and more accurately than state-of-the-art baseline methods on two real-world datasets.
_summary.methodology: Model microblog diffusion as propagation trees and compute similarities between trees using the proposed propagation tree kernel (PTK) and context-sensitive PTK (cPTK); use these kernels in a kernel SVM (one-vs-all) to classify source tweets into non-rumor, false rumor, true rumor, or unverified rumor.
limitations: Authors state there is no public dataset for classifying propagation trees and therefore construct their own datasets.
The study ignores unpopular source tweets (they focus on highly retweeted or replied source tweets).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - rumor/misinformation detection on social media

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Tenured professor in machine learning and formal methods with a track record of kernel methods and learning theory work; frequently reviews for NeurIPS and ICML.
traits: Penalizes papers that introduce a new model/metric without formal properties or theoretical guarantees (e.g., consistency, representational limits, computational complexity) — expects at least one formal proposition or theorem about the proposed kernel.

Requires comparison of proposed method to a simple rigorous oracle or ablation that isolates the claimed novelty; subtracts points if the paper lacks controlled ablations that clearly separate propagation-structure modeling from other cues.
Demands analysis of worst-case/time complexity and scalability; deducts points if no empirical runtime scaling or complexity bound is provided for training/inference on realistic graph sizes.

Skeptical of purely empirical claims on two datasets; penalizes papers that do not include a discussion of dataset biases, statistical significance tests, or robustness checks (e.g., varying sizes, noisy inputs).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes formal clarity, theoretical justification, and controlled ablations over empirical improvements; will accept empirical results only if accompanied by clear theoretical or algorithmic analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in applied NLP and social media mining with experience deploying misinformation-detection systems; regular reviewer for ACL and EMNLP.
traits: Rewards papers that demonstrate clear empirical improvements on real-world benchmarks and multiple meaningful baselines; expects at least three competitive baselines (including non-structure baselines and prior propagation-based methods).
Values practicality and early-detection performance; boosts score if the method can detect rumors earlier or with lower-latency inputs and provides runtime/efficiency evidence on realistic datasets.

Favors methods that model task-relevant structure (e.g., propagation trees) and provides qualitative analyses (case studies, example trees) showing interpretable behavior; deducts points if no qualitative/interpretability discussion is present.
Prefers reproducibility: awards points when authors release code or provide clear hyperparameter/feature details; penalizes omission of training details or dataset splits.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes real-world impact, practicality, and strong empirical validation; willing to accept modest theoretical grounding if the experiments and qualitative analyses convincingly show usefulness.
